skill.name: powershell-windows
skill.description: PowerShell Windows patterns and syntax reference for safe scripting practices
skill.summary: A comprehensive reference guide for PowerShell Windows scripting patterns, covering operator syntax, error handling, and common pitfalls
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 34 patterns but all are false positives. The SKILL.md file is documentation only, containing PowerShell syntax examples and best practices. No executable code or security risks present. The skill is a reference guide for proper PowerShell scripting patterns on Windows.

content.value_statement: Avoid common PowerShell pitfalls that cause script failures. Learn proper operator syntax, error handling, and Windows-specific patterns for reliable automation.
actual_capabilities: Explain PowerShell operator syntax rules with parentheses requirements
Provide null check patterns for safe variable access
Demonstrate proper error handling with try-catch-finally blocks
Guide Windows path handling with Join-Path for cross-platform safety
Show JSON operations with correct depth parameters
Provide script templates following strict mode best practices
limitations: Documentation reference only, does not execute PowerShell commands
Focused on Windows PowerShell patterns, limited Linux coverage
Does not cover advanced PowerShell modules or DSC configurations

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Scripting
use_cases.description: Create reliable Windows automation scripts with proper error handling and null safety for CI/CD pipelines
use_cases.target_user: DevOps engineers building Windows deployment automation

use_cases.title: System Administrator Reference
use_cases.description: Quick reference for PowerShell syntax rules and common pitfalls when maintaining legacy scripts
use_cases.target_user: IT administrators managing Windows server environments

use_cases.title: Developer Learning Resource
use_cases.description: Learn PowerShell best practices and avoid common mistakes when adding Windows scripting to your toolkit

prompt_templates.title: Basic Syntax Question
prompt_templates.prompt: What is the correct PowerShell syntax for checking if a file exists OR another condition is true?

prompt_templates.title: Error Handling Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the proper try-catch-finally pattern for PowerShell scripts that need to clean up resources
prompt_templates.scenario: Writing production-ready scripts with error recovery

prompt_templates.title: Null Safety Check
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I safely check if an array has items before accessing its Count property in PowerShell?
prompt_templates.scenario: Avoiding null reference exceptions in scripts

prompt_templates.title: JSON File Operations
prompt_templates.prompt: What is the correct way to read and write JSON files in PowerShell with nested objects?
prompt_templates.scenario: Working with configuration files and API responses

output_examples.input: Check if file exists or directory exists
output_examples.output: Use: if ((Test-Path "file.txt") -or (Test-Path "folder")) - Each cmdlet must be wrapped in parentheses when using logical operators
Use: if ($array -and $array.Count -gt 0) - Always verify the array exists before accessing properties
Use: $data | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10 - Always specify depth parameter for nested objects to avoid truncation

best_practices: Wrap each cmdlet in parentheses when using logical operators like -or and -and
Always check for null before accessing object properties or methods
Use Join-Path for building file paths instead of string concatenation
Set ErrorActionPreference appropriately for your environment (Stop for dev, Continue for production)
Specify -Depth parameter when converting nested objects to JSON
anti_patterns: Using unicode or emoji characters in PowerShell scripts - use ASCII markers like [OK] instead
Accessing properties without null checks - will cause runtime errors on null values
Using ConvertTo-Json without -Depth parameter - nested objects will be truncated
Placing return statements inside try blocks - use finally for cleanup and return after

faq.question: Why do PowerShell logical operators require extra parentheses?
faq.answer: PowerShell parses cmdlets and operators differently than other languages. Each cmdlet call must be wrapped in parentheses so the parser correctly identifies where each command ends before applying the logical operator.

faq.question: What is the safest way to handle file paths in PowerShell?
faq.answer: Use Join-Path instead of string concatenation. This handles path separators correctly across different Windows versions and avoids issues with trailing backslashes.

faq.question: Why should I avoid unicode characters in PowerShell scripts?
faq.answer: Unicode characters can cause parsing errors on systems with different encodings. Using ASCII markers like [OK], [WARN], or [ERROR] ensures compatibility across all Windows environments.

faq.question: What ErrorActionPreference should I use in production?
faq.answer: Use 'Continue' in production to allow scripts to handle errors gracefully. Use 'Stop' during development to catch issues early. Use 'SilentlyContinue' when errors are expected and handled.

faq.question: How do I properly convert nested objects to JSON?
faq.answer: Always specify the -Depth parameter with ConvertTo-Json. The default depth is 2, which truncates nested objects. Use -Depth 10 or higher for deeply nested structures.

faq.question: What is the correct pattern for null-safe array access?
faq.answer: Check both the array and its count: if ($array -and $array.Count -gt 0). This prevents errors when the array is null or undefined before accessing the Count property.
